Representative_Docs: ["Hundreds protest in Tunisia over president's escalating crackdown on freedoms TUNIS, Tunisia (AP) — Tunisians took to the streets of downtown Tunis on Saturday over what they described as President Kais Saied ’s increasingly authoritarian rule and demanding the release of all jailed political prisoners. The rally, held under the banner “Against Injustice,” brought together families of political detainees and activists from different ideological backgrounds. The demonstration drew more than 1,000 protesters, some dressed in black and chanting anti-regime slogans, including “The people want to overthrow the regime,” “What a great country! Oppression and tyranny!” and “No fear no terror, the street belongs to the people.” The protest came as part of a broader surge in protests nationwide over political and economic turmoil under Saied’s rule. On Thursday, Tunisian journalists protested against the widening crackdown on the freedom of the press and the temporary suspension of several prominent civil society organizations. Protesters also called out Saied’s interference in the judiciary and accused him of using the police to target political opponents. Name: 55_surgical_surgeons_surgery_minimally invasive
Representative_Docs: ['VIDEO: $5.5M robotic surgery system arrives at Surrey Memorial Hospital Published 12:10 pm Tuesday, January 27, 2026 By Anna Burns The da Vinci Xi System has been unpacked and calibrated and is being held in a temporary location until its permanent location is finished. Fraser Health said that it expects it to “go-live” later in the spring. (Fraser Health/ Contributed to Surrey Now-Leader) Health care workers pose in front of the da Vinci Xi System. (Fraser Health/ Contributed to Surrey Now-Leader) Surrey Memorial Hospital is one step closer to providing robotic-assisted surgery with the arrival of the da Vinci Xi Robotic Surgery system. It is expected to be used in surgeries in the spring. “With the aid of the da Vinci Xi system, we can expand minimally invasive thoracic and foregut surgery, and we can do operations with greater precision and accuracy,” says Dr Ahmad Ashrafi, regional division head and chief of thoracic surgery for Fraser Health. “Patients may experience quicker recovery times, and we can carry out more complex procedures right here in Surrey, using minimally invasive techniques. Name: 56_santa_christmas_holiday season_festive
Representative_Docs: ['Santa Claus is coming to Grafton: VFW Post 3081 Auxiliary hosts Breakfast with Santa Families will be welcome to breakfast with Santa on Saturday, Dec. 6, from 9-11 a.m., at the VFW Post 3081 Post Home, located at 225 West Main St., Grafton. Reservations are not needed to take part in this cherished holiday tradition. GRAFTON, W.Va. — Christmas is fast approaching, and Santa Claus is taking a break from holiday preparations at the North Pole to visit Grafton for a special breakfast. The Veterans of Foreign Wars (VFW) Post 3081 Auxiliary has announced it will be hosting this festive event for the area’s children, giving them a chance to meet Santa. “This cherished holiday tradition is all about spreading Christmas joy and giving kids a chance to share their wishes with Santa,” said VFW member Amanda Bise. “Enjoy a hearty breakfast in a festive atmosphere while soaking in the spirit of the season.” Bise On Saturday, December 6, families are invited to the post home, located at 225 West Main Street in Grafton, for a delicious breakfast designed to create magical memories. The breakfast will be held from 9 to 11 a.m. The cost for the breakfast is just $5 per adult, and children will eat for free. “Bring your little ones to meet Santa Claus before he heads back to the N', 'Want an ‘ezbacke uvin’ or ‘mot cantrl’ for Christmas? Write to Santa The time has come again for both the young and old to send in their letters to Santa. This has become one of my favorite Christmas traditions. The letters to Santa we publish each December are often heartwarming and occasionally heartbreaking. They are a reminder of what makes Christmas magical. They are an amusing break from the sometimes jarring news we must print in the paper. And, hey, they might even be beneficial to your brain’s elasticity. It takes some creativity on the reader’s part to figure out just what the little kiddies are asking for, since we publish the letters verbatim. Try to decipher these phrases from previous Santa letters: I’m looking forward to the, umm, creative writing in this year’s batch of letters. We will publish Santa letters from children and adults in north central Idaho and southeastern Washington. The letters can be just a few words if you have a short wish list, but please try to write no more than 300 words. Be sure to include the author’s name, age and town of residence. The letters can be emailed to city@lmtribune.com, sent in the mail to Letters to Santa, Lewiston Tribune, 505 Capital St., Lewiston, ID 83501, or dropped off at our office. Name: 57_deer hunting_hunting_deer_state parks
Representative_Docs: ['Mid-season WMA waterfowl quota hunt app period to be held Oct. 22-Nov. 12 TWRA News Tennessee Wildlife Resources Agency (TWRA) announced that the application period for the 2025 Mid-Season Wildlife Management Area (WMA) Waterfowl Quota Hunt will be open from Wednesday, Oct. 22 through Wednesday, Nov. 12. Applications can be submitted online at gooutdoorstennessee.com, through the TWRA mobile app, at TWRA regional offices, or at any TWRA license agent. Mailed applications will not be accepted, and applicants must be at least 16 years old. Detailed application instructions, WMA locations, and general waterfowl quota hunt information are available on the TWRA Quota Hunts webpage. The deadline to submit applications is Wednesday, Nov. 12 at 11:59 p.m. (CST). A person may only submit one application, which can include up to 48 unique hunt choices; hunt choices may not be repeated. Qualifying parties must have at least four members, with a maximum of eight. Successful applicants will receive an email with instructions to log in and must claim their permit by Monday, Nov. 24 at 11:59 p.m. CST. Each party member must claim their own permit. Name: 58_garden_flower_flowers_gardeners
Representative_Docs: ['Attract bees and butterflies to garden with ‘superplant’ bursting with nectar-rich flowers Gardeners can attract bees and butterflies into their outdoor spaces with a nectar-rich "superplant" beloved by wildlife. As spring continues to unfold, green-fingered readers will be heading outdoors to tend to their gardens—made all the more rewarding when wildlife appears to enjoy the results. Pollinators like bees and butterflies play a crucial role in our ecosystem, supporting the growth of plants, fruit and vegetables. They\'re also synonymous with summer and a joy to watch as they flit from flower to flower. The Royal Horticultural Society (RHS) has named a "striking and versatile" wildflower that can help tempt these valuable insects into your own garden, calling it a "top plant we can all grow to support biodiversity". We use your sign-up to provide content in ways you\'ve consented to and to improve our understanding of you. This may include adverts from us and 3rd parties based on our understanding. You can unsubscribe at any time. Read our Privacy Policy READ MORE: UK gardeners urged not to make simple mistake over next two weeks READ MORE: Households urged to scatter sweetcorn on garden tables and lawns in spring In a recent piece about the so-called "superplant", Olivia', "Montebello Park's rose garden gets plaque honouring William Burgoyne's efforts Mayor Mat Siscoe and Jan Harte, St. Catharines Horticultural Society, now the Garden City Gardeners past president, unveil the plaque honouring William Bartless Burgoyne for his efforts in starting Canada’s first civic rose garden in St. Catharines. Montebello Park was abuzz as the St. Catharines Horticultural Society and the City unveiled an interpretive panel to honour William Bartlett Burgoyne for his efforts in funding Canada’s first civic rose garden. Burgoyne, a past President of the St. Catharines Horticultural Society, now the Garden City Gardeners, and founder of the St. Catharines Standard started the rose garden with a donation of $1,000 in 1919, an equivalent of over $19,000 in 2026. Tucked in the back of Montebello Park, near the intersection of Lake and Queen streets, the rose garden held a long-kept secret, until a St. Catharines Horticultural Society member revealed it. What started as an interest quickly became a desire to honour the past president, as Jan Harte, recent past president, investigated the garden’s history. “I’ve been very interested in the rose garden, it’s a beautiful centre piece for the park and for the city,” she said. “Then when I found out it wa", "Montebello Park's rose garden gets plaque honouring William Burgoyne's efforts Mayor Mat Siscoe and Jan Harte, St. Catharines Horticultural Society, now the Garden City Gardeners past president, unveil the plaque honouring William Bartless Burgoyne for his efforts in starting Canada’s first civic rose garden in St. Catharines.
